name: Science Thinking Realism and Model Comparison Artifact
population_or_context: OpenSciEd science lesson ideas across life sciences, physics, and chemistry, spanning elementary, middle, and high school lessons.
sample_size: 49 lesson plans; 30 simulated ideas per lesson per LLM; 8,820 generated ideas across six LLMs; six teacher interviews for utility perceptions.
methods: Six LLMs generated possible student ideas from structured lesson-context prompts. The study evaluated semantic similarity to lesson/student ideas, grade-level language, knowledge scope, qualitative divergence, and teacher perceptions of usefulness.
education_context: K-12 science instruction planning and responsive teaching with open-ended student scientific ideas.
response_origin: LLM-generated simulated student ideas from six commercial and open/open-source model families.
rubric_label_space: Realism and instructional-planning fit metrics rather than an automated scoring rubric.
label_source: OpenSciEd lesson ideas based on pilot student data and teacher consultation provide the main comparator; teacher interviews provide use-case judgments.
generation_model: GPT-5-mini, GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Mistral 7B, and Llama-4-17B.
generation_method: Structured prompting with learning standards, prior instruction, activity description, task description, grade level, domain, and instructions to include everyday, scientific, partial, and incorrect reasoning.
prompt_strategy: Lesson-context prompt with explicit simulation instructions for language and reasoning style.
evaluation_method: Cluster-level embedding cosine similarity, language appropriateness, knowledge-scope judgment, qualitative divergence analysis, and interviews with teachers.
scorer_use: No automated scorer validation; simulated responses support teacher anticipation and instructional planning.
real_response_comparator: Lesson ideas from OpenSciEd, which the paper describes as generated from pilot implementation and teacher consultation, serve as the comparator for generated student ideas.
reported_metrics: Mean cosine similarity across generated and lesson ideas was 0.52 (SD = 0.07); larger/proprietary models exceeded Mistral-7B on similarity, and grade-level differences were observed.
limitations: The use case is instructional planning, not automated score validation.
Semantic similarity and teacher-perceived realism do not establish interchangeability with real student responses.
The comparator is lesson-plan student ideas rather than a fully independent operational response sample for scoring validation.
